content: [{"section_id": "s01", "title": "Fundamentals of Damp Heat Aging in Polymeric Encapsulants", "description": "Defines the damp heat aging mechanism where elevated temperature increases chemical reaction rates and moisture diffusion coefficients, causing synergistic degradation of adhesive joints. Covers Arrhenius temperature dependence of adhesion degradation and the distinction between reversible (moisture-driven) and irreversible (hydrolysis, microcrack) effects."}, {"section_id": "s02", "title": "Adhesion Degradation Kinetics at Encapsulant-Glass and Encapsulant-Cell Interfaces", "description": "Examines quantitative peel force and adhesion energy reductions under DH aging: EVA adhesion falling to 15-year field-equivalent levels at 10,000h accelerated aging, TPO peel forces dropping from 160 to 33-45 N/cm after DH3000, and differential degradation rates between glass/EVA and EVA/cell interfaces."}, {"section_id": "s03", "title": "Encapsulant Material Comparison: EVA, POE, TPO, and EPE under Damp Heat Stress", "description": "Compares adhesion stability of EVA, POE, EPE, and TPO encapsulants under hot-dry and hot-humid conditions at coupon and module level. POE demonstrates best adhesion stability under thermal aging; EVA shows less peel force degradation than POE after DH; failure mode transitions from interfacial to combined cohesive-interfacial observed in POE."}, {"section_id": "s04", "title": "Influence of Vinyl Acetate Content and Crosslinking Degree on DH Resistance", "description": "Analyzes the effect of VA content (18-40%) and gel content (optimal 84-90%) on encapsulant mechanical integrity after DH aging. Lower VA content (24%) improves tensile strength and crosslinking retention; gel content above 92% leads to yellowing and unstable adhesion; a crosslinking saturation level exists above which further gel content has negligible effect."}, {"section_id": "s05", "title": "Lamination Process Parameters and Their Effect on Long-Term Adhesion Retention", "description": "Evaluates how lamination temperature (140-180 degrees C), curing time, and encapsulant thickness affect peel force retention after DH aging up to 3000h. Higher lamination temperatures are beneficial for crosslinking encapsulants; RMPF ranges from 15.7% to 73.0% depending on material and process; cost-cutting measures (thinner encapsulant, shorter lamination) are linked to field failures."}, {"section_id": "s06", "title": "Moisture Diffusion, Hydrolytic Degradation, and Interfacial Chemistry", "description": "Details moisture ingress mechanisms following Fick diffusion law, with saturated absorption of 14-16% in epoxy adhesives at 80 degrees C/95% RH. Covers XPS evidence of carboxylic acid groups on fractured encapsulant surfaces and deprotonated silanol bands on glass, indicating hydrolytic degradation of silane coupling agent bonds at the interface."}, {"section_id": "s07", "title": "Industry Standards, Quality Thresholds, and Field Failure Rates", "description": "Reviews IEC 61215 and IEC 61730 DH test requirements (85 degrees C/85% RH, 1000-2000h), minimum adhesion thresholds (53 N/cm per STR, 160 J/m2 debonding energy per Tracy et al.), and 2024 PVEL scorecard data showing DH failure rates doubling year-over-year with 11% of BOMs failing due to delamination."}, {"section_id": "s08", "title": "Implications for DVS-PEI Encapsulant Design in Flexible Electronics", "description": "Synthesizes findings to identify design criteria for DVS-PEI adhesion durability: target elastic modulus range (7-20 MPa to avoid CTE-mismatch delamination), crosslinking optimization, moisture barrier strategies, and relevance of PV encapsulant aging data to flexible electronics operating under similar damp heat conditions."}]
